engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: You could take the checking queen — and blocked instead
mistakes.explanation: You were winning outright: +4.7, a clean piece up after White's opening fell apart. Then 8.Qb5+ gave a check that looks scary but hangs the queen — your own queen on d5 covers b5. 8…Qxb5! just takes it. After 9.Bxb5+ c6 the recapturing bishop is hit by your pawn and has nowhere to run, so 10.Nxa3 cxb5 leaves you a whole piece up with an easy game — a capture 40% of players at your level find. Instead 8…c6 blocked the check with the pawn, and 9.Qxd5! cxd5 traded the queens off and let White win back the a3-bishop with 10.Nxa3. Your extra piece evaporated and +4.7 became +1. The check was loud, but the checking piece was hanging: before you block a check, always ask whether you can just take the checker.

takeaways.lesson: When you are in check, look at capturing the checker first.
takeaways.detail: Make it a fixed reflex: on every check, the first candidate is always “can I take the piece giving check?” Only if that fails do you consider blocking or moving the king. Here Qxb5 was legal and simply won.

takeaways.lesson: A queen that gives a loud check can still be hanging.
takeaways.detail: Scary-looking moves are the ones we react to instead of calculate. Slow down on every check and count the defenders of the checking square — b5 was defended by nothing, your queen attacked it.

mistakes.title: The bishop on g4 you never let go
mistakes.explanation: You are comfortably better here (+1.4), and 10…O-O is perfectly natural. But 10…Bxf3! 11.gxf3 was stronger: it trades your awkward light-squared bishop for the knight and shatters White's kingside pawns, leaving you a clean, simple edge. This wasn't a one-off — Bxf3 was the engine's first choice on six of your moves (moves 6, 10, 11, 13, 15 and 17), and each time the bishop stayed on g4 doing nothing. A piece with no job and no safe retreat is a piece to trade while you still set the terms. You didn't, and on move 23 a knight landed on e5 and took the bishop for free — the loose piece finally dropped off.

takeaways.lesson: Trade a passive piece before it becomes a target.
takeaways.detail: Once a piece stops helping and has no good square, look to exchange it on your terms. Each turn, glance at your worst-placed piece and ask whether a trade improves you.

takeaways.lesson: A capture that doubles the enemy's pawns is worth a hard look.
takeaways.detail: Bxf3 wrecking White's g/f pawns is a structural win, not just a trade. Whenever you can capture toward the enemy king's pawns, calculate it before dismissing it.

mistakes.title: 16…Ra8 — a rook that walked away from the game
mistakes.explanation: The position was balanced (+0.6) and full of tension: your b6-pawn stares down White's c5-pawn, and there are real moves. 16…bxc5 resolves it in your favour, and even 16…Bxf3 (that bishop again) keeps things level. Instead 16…Ra8 tucked a rook into the corner where it did nothing, and 17.cxb6! crashed the pawn through — a protected passer on b6 and your edge flipped from +0.6 to −2.9 in one move. Only about 1% of players at your level would pick Ra8: this wasn't a gap in knowledge, it was a move made without a plan. Before a quiet-looking move, ask what it actually accomplishes.

takeaways.lesson: In a balanced position, resolve the tension instead of shuffling.
takeaways.detail: When pawns face off (your b6 vs c5), a passive waiting move often just lets the opponent choose the moment. Decide the tension yourself — here 16…bxc5 kept the initiative.

takeaways.lesson: Give every move a one-line reason before you play it.
takeaways.detail: “This rook goes to a8 to do ___.” If you can't finish the sentence, the move is probably drifting. Ra8 had no answer; bxc5 and Bxf3 both did.

highlights.note: 7…Bxa3 snapped off White's dark-squared bishop and seized the initiative — the engine's top move and one only 15% of players at your level find here. Taking what the position offers is exactly the right instinct; the trick is holding onto it two moves later, when the whole queen was available.

14…Nc6 calmly dropped the attacked knight back to its best square rather than lashing out — the engine's choice, found by 19% of your level. Quiet, correct regrouping under pressure is a real skill; trust it more often than the flashy alternative.

openingReport.note: White's 2.Qf3 is an offbeat, unbooked move — you were on your own from move 2 and handled it well. The one opening moment that decided everything was move 8: 8.Qb5+ actually hung material, and 8…Qxb5 keeps you a clean piece up (winning chances hold near 85%), but 8…c6 let White trade queens and slip back to near-equality (85% → 60%).
